source_paper_title: A Characterization of Simultaneous Optimization, Majorization, and (Bi-)Submodular Polyhedra
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Schoot_Uiterkamp_2025, title={A Characterization of Simultaneous Optimization, Majorization, and (Bi-)Submodular Polyhedra}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0054}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0054}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Schoot Uiterkamp, Martijn H. H.}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={252–276} }
context:
Page 25, Section 7 (Conclusions and directions for future research), item 1 in the numbered list of future directions.

One limitation of this work is that we focused on the case where least \((a, b)\)-majorized elements exist for each choice of \(a\) and \(b\). Moreover, the definition of these elements requires them to be optimal for any choice of separable convex objective structure. Thus, it is interesting to investigate whether the existence of least \((a, b)\)-majorized elements for specific (sets of) values of \(a\) and \(b\) can be characterized. In particular, an interesting question is whether such a characterization exists for \(b = 0\), i.e., when we only consider scaled objective functions.
title: Characterize least (a,b)-majorized elements for fixed scalings and shifts in feasible sets
background_latex:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and index set \(N:=\{1,\dots,n\}\). For \(x\in\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(S\subseteq N\), write \(x(S):=\sum_{i\in S} x_i\). Fix a scaling vector \(a\in\mathbb{R}^n_{>0}\) and a shift vector \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\).

For vectors \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}^n\), define \((a,b)\)-majorization by
\[
 x \prec_{(a,b)} y \quad\text{if}\quad x(N)=y(N)\ \text{and}\ \sum_{i\in N} a_i\,\phi\!\left(\frac{x_i+b_i}{a_i}\right)\le \sum_{i\in N} a_i\,\phi\!\left(\frac{y_i+b_i}{a_i}\right)\ \text{for all continuous convex }\phi:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}.
\]
Given a feasible set \(C\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\), a vector \(x^*\in C\) is called a least \((a,b)\)-majorized element of \(C\) if \(x^*\prec_{(a,b)} y\) for all \(y\in C\). Equivalently, \(x^*\) simultaneously minimizes the family of separable convex objectives
\[
 x\mapsto \sum_{i\in N} a_i\,\phi\!\left(\frac{x_i+b_i}{a_i}\right)
\]
over \(C\), for every choice of continuous convex \(\phi\).

A major theme in the paper is that if one requires existence of least \((a,b)\)-majorized elements for all choices of \(a\in\mathbb{R}^n_{>0}\) and \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\) within the class of compact convex sets, then the feasible sets are precisely base polyhedra of submodular functions (and analogous characterizations for weaker notions lead to other polyhedra). The paper explicitly notes a limitation: it treats the case of existence for every \(a,b\), rather than for specific fixed \(a,b\) (or subsets thereof).
problem_definition_latex:
Given a feasible set \(C\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) and fixed vectors \(a\in\mathbb{R}^n_{>0}\) and \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\), characterize (in structural/combinatorial terms) when \(C\) admits a least \((a,b)\)-majorized element, i.e., when there exists \(x^*\in C\) such that
\[
\sum_{i\in N} a_i\,\phi\!\left(\frac{x_i^*+b_i}{a_i}\right)\le \sum_{i\in N} a_i\,\phi\!\left(\frac{y_i+b_i}{a_i}\right)\quad\text{for all }y\in C\text{ and all continuous convex }\phi:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R},
\]
(with the implicit requirement \(x^*(N)=y(N)\) for all \(y\in C\) whenever \((a,b)\)-majorization is used).
In particular, investigate whether such a characterization exists in the special case \(b=0\) (scaled objectives only).

Given a feasible set \(C\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\) and fixed vectors \(a\in\mathbb{R}^n_{>0}\) and \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\), characterize (in structural/combinatorial terms) when \(C\) admits a least \((a,b)\)-majorized element, i.e., when there exists \(x^*\in C\) such that

location_in_paper: Page 25, Section 7 (Conclusions and directions for future research), item 1 in the numbered list of future directions.
open_problem_quote: One limitation of this work is that we focused on the case where least \((a, b)\)-majorized elements exist for each choice of \(a\) and \(b\). Moreover, the definition of these elements requires them to be optimal for any choice of separable convex objective structure. Thus, it is interesting to investigate whether the existence of least \((a, b)\)-majorized elements for specific (sets of) values of \(a\) and \(b\) can be characterized. In particular, an interesting question is whether such a characterization exists for \(b = 0\), i.e., when we only consider scaled objective functions.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for an existential/structural characterization of feasible sets, which is not naturally reducible to searching finite instances for a counterexample to a concrete conjectured inequality. Automated search could generate examples of sets lacking least \((a,b)\)-majorized elements, but that would not directly yield the desired characterization.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate families of sets (e.g., parametrized polytopes) and numerically test whether a least \((a,b)\)-majorized element exists by solving a suite of convex programs for many convex functions \(\phi\) (approximated via a basis of piecewise-linear convex functions). This could help discover patterns and formulate conjectured structural conditions, but the final goal is a theorem-level characterization.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Schoot Uiterkamp, 2023) introduces \((a,b)\)-majorization and proves strong uniform-in-\((a,b)\) characterizations: among compact convex sets, admitting a least \((a,b)\)-majorized element for every \(a>0\) and \(b\) is equivalent to being a base polyhedron \(B(f)\) of a submodular function; analogous uniform statements for weak sub/super and weak absolute majorization yield (super/sub)modular and bisubmodular polyhedra. These results rely on separable convex minimization over base polyhedra (via exchange/greedy structure) and on reductions to max-function test families for \((a,b)\)-majorization.

The open problem asks for a structural/combinatorial characterization when \(a\) and \(b\) are fixed (especially \(b=0\)). Existing literature provides ingredients but not a complete answer: Veinott’s d-majorization (the \((a,0)\) case) and classical majorization theory (Marshall–Olkin–Arnold) offer alternative characterizations (e.g., via convex tests or stochastic-matrix formulations) that might be adapted to fixed \(a\). On the feasible-set side, work on least-majorization phenomena for generalized polymatroids (Tamir) and the broader submodular-optimization toolkit (Fujishige) suggest that for fixed parameters the class of sets admitting a least element may be substantially larger and may depend delicately on how \(C\) intersects level sets of \(x(N)\) and on exchange directions available in \(C\).

At present, there is no forward-citation evidence of a solved characterization for fixed \((a,b)\); the problem appears open. Promising directions include: (i) deriving necessary and sufficient conditions in terms of the normal fan/face lattice of \(C\) relative to the family of separable convex objectives \(\sum_i a_i\phi((x_i+b_i)/a_i)\); (ii) specializing to polyhedra with known exchange properties (e.g., generalized polymatroids, jump systems) to see when a fixed \((a,b)\) least element exists; and (iii) for \(b=0\), exploiting the d-majorization viewpoint to express \((a,0)\)-majorization via a weighted doubly stochastic order, potentially yielding a characterization in terms of invariance/monotonicity of \(C\) under the corresponding averaging operations.

sources.title: A characterization of simultaneous optimization, majorization, and (bi)submodular polyhedra
sources.theorem_or_result:
Introduces 
\((a,b)\)-majorization and proves that among compact convex sets, having least 
\((a,b)\)-majorized elements for all 
a>0,b\) characterizes base polyhedra; analogous characterizations for weak sub/super and weak absolute majorization yield (super/sub)modular and bisubmodular polyhedra.
sources.connection:
This is the source paper posing the limitation: it characterizes existence uniformly over all 
\(a,b\), but does not characterize existence for a fixed given 
\(a,b\) (notably 
\(b=0\)).

sources.title: Least d-majorized network flows with inventory and statistical applications
sources.theorem_or_result:
Introduces d-majorization (equivalent to 
\((d,0)\)-majorization) and studies least d-majorized flows in network settings, linking majorization-type orders to convex separable objectives.
sources.connection:
Provides the closest classical antecedent for the fixed-scaling case 
\(b=0\): understanding when least 
\((a,0)\)-majorized elements exist may build on Veinott's d-majorization framework.

sources.title: Least majorized elements and generalized polymatroids
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows existence/structure of least (classical) weakly sub- and supermajorized elements for bounded generalized polymatroids, extending least-majorization phenomena beyond base polyhedra for weaker orders.
sources.connection:
Suggests that for fixed 
\((a,b)\) and weaker notions, feasible sets broader than base polyhedra can admit least elements; may inform partial characterizations for fixed parameters.

sources.title: Simultaneous optimization via approximate majorization for concave profits or convex costs
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops approximate simultaneous optimization guarantees using approximate majorization, giving algorithms that approximately optimize families of separable convex/concave objectives.
sources.connection:
If exact least 
\((a,b)\)-majorized elements for fixed 
\((a,b)\) are hard to characterize, this work motivates studying approximate variants parameterized by 
\((a,b)\).

sources.title: Inequalities: Theory of Majorization and Its Applications
sources.theorem_or_result: Comprehensive reference on majorization, Schur-convexity, and equivalent characterizations (convex separable tests, doubly stochastic matrices, etc.).
sources.connection:
Provides foundational tools for analyzing 
\((a,b)\)-majorization for fixed parameters, e.g., via reduction to max-function tests and potential matrix characterizations in special cases.

sources.title: Submodular Functions and Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: Standard reference on submodular functions, base polyhedra, greedy/extreme point structure, and separable convex minimization over base polyhedra.
sources.connection:
Any structural characterization for fixed 
\((a,b)\) likely uses base-polyhedron machinery (exchange properties, separable convex minimization) developed here.
